topic: Simran's dal makhani recipe — she changed something

turns.content: ok so Simran made dal makhani last night and something was different. like it was still good but it wasn't THE dal makhani. she changed something and she won't tell me what

The mystery recipe change — that's a serious situation. When you say something was different, can you describe what specifically tasted off? Was it the texture, the spice level, the richness? That might help narrow down what she switched up.

it was less rich I think. like normally her dal makhani is proper thick and creamy, the kind where you can feel it sitting in your stomach for hours in a good way. this one was lighter. still tasty but not the same heaviness. and the colour was a bit different too, lighter brown

Based on what you're describing — less rich, lighter texture, lighter colour — my guess is she cut back on the cream or butter, or possibly swapped regular cream for something lighter. Traditional dal makhani gets that thick, dark richness from generous amounts of butter and cream simmered in for a long time. If you reduce that or substitute it, you'd get exactly what you're describing.

The lighter colour is a giveaway too. The deep dark brown comes from long slow cooking of the dal with butter. Less fat or shorter cooking time would keep it lighter.

Has she been on any kind of health kick recently? That's usually what drives the "secret recipe change."

YES she has actually. she's been going on about reducing fat and cholesterol and all that. she probably swapped the cream for something. I didn't say anything at dinner cos she worked hard on it but mate I could tell straight away. you can't mess with dal makhani innit

Ha, classic — a health-motivated stealth recipe change. She probably thought nobody would notice, but you can't sneak reduced cream past someone who grew up on Punjabi cooking.

If she swapped heavy cream for something like yogurt, low-fat cream, or even coconut cream, that would explain both the lighter colour and the thinner body. Some people also swap butter for oil, which removes that distinctive makhani richness entirely.

Here's the diplomatic approach: instead of saying "it was different" (which sounds like criticism), you could say "that was really good, but I missed that extra richness from your usual version." Frames it as a compliment to the original rather than a complaint about the new one. Then maybe suggest a compromise — slightly less cream than usual rather than a full substitution.

haha that's proper diplomatic that is. you're right though I don't want to be rude about it cos she's a brilliant cook. the thing is her mum's dal makhani is unreal and that recipe was passed down so if Simran's changing it I feel like that's sacred territory innit

You're touching on something real there — family recipes carry weight that goes beyond just taste. It's tradition, it's memory, it's identity. So I get why it feels like sacred territory.

But here's the thing: every generation tweaks recipes. Her mum probably changed something from her mum's version too. The recipe evolves but the heart of it stays the same. If Simran's adjusting for health reasons, she's not disrespecting the recipe — she's trying to keep making it for another thirty years without clogged arteries.

Maybe the compromise is: health version for weeknights, full-fat original for special occasions. That way the traditional recipe stays alive and nobody's doctor is worried.

that's actually a solid plan. weeknight version and a proper full fat weekend version. I'm gonna pitch that to her. the kids won't care either way honestly, Arjun just drowns everything in ketchup which is a crime and Preet only eats it with about 10 rotis

Ketchup on dal makhani — Arjun is committing a culinary offence there, no question. And Preet using the dal as basically a roti delivery system is very on brand for a five-year-old.

The weeknight/weekend split is a good pitch. You could even frame it as "let's do the full recipe on Sundays as a family tradition" — makes it sound like a nice ritual rather than you just wanting more butter. Strategic framing is everything here.

mate you're good at this. 'family tradition' framing, she'll love that. she's a sucker for anything that sounds like quality family time. right I'm gonna bring it up tonight. wish me luck cos if she thinks I'm criticising her cooking I'm sleeping on the sofa

Lead with the compliment, pitch the Sunday tradition angle, do NOT mention that you could tell something was different. If she asks, you loved it, you just had an idea about making Sundays special. Plausible deniability is your friend here.

Good luck — and if it goes well, you'll be eating proper full-fat dal makhani by this Sunday. Report back on whether the diplomatic mission was a success.

haha plausible deniability, love that. you know what the funny thing is, if I just straight up said 'babe I noticed you changed the recipe' she'd probably respect that more but there's no way I'm risking it innit. the sofa is well uncomfortable

Honestly, you know your wife better than anyone, so trust your instincts on the approach. But yeah, the sofa risk is real — no dal makhani is worth a bad night's sleep.

Just remember: compliment first, suggest the Sunday idea second, and if she sees through it and asks if you noticed the change, just smile and say "I thought it was great but I had this idea." Keep it light and you'll be fine.

sorted. I'll keep it casual and breezy. honestly the worst case scenario is she says no and we keep eating the lighter version which is still brilliant food at the end of the day. alright let me go strategise over a cup of tea. cheers for the help

That's the right attitude — worst case you're still eating your wife's excellent cooking, just with slightly less cream. Not exactly a hardship.

Good luck with the tea-fuelled strategy session. Let me know how it goes — I'm invested in the outcome of this dal makhani negotiation now.
